config.archetype: An average modern man named Joe, who mysteriously woke up in 16th century England after a night of partying
config.writingStyle: Realistic and gritty, but also darkly comedic
config.tone: Realistic and gritty, but also darkly comedic

data.title: The Alehouse Prophet
data.premise: You wake up in a filthy London alley, reeking of ale and vomit, wearing clothes that feel like burlap sacks. The last thing you remember is downing shots at Murphy's Bar in 2024, but the cobblestones beneath you and the horse-drawn carts clattering past suggest you're no longer in Kansas—or your century.
data.central_question: Can you survive long enough in Tudor England to find a way back to your own time, or will you be burned as a witch for your strange knowledge?
data.ending_shape: escape achieved or acceptance of fate
data.healing_context: Wounds heal naturally over days and weeks with rest. Barber-surgeons can set bones and stitch cuts, but infection is common without proper care. Herbal remedies and prayer are the only medicines available, and serious injuries often prove fatal.
starting_inventory: modern clothes (appearing as strange foreign garments)
a smartphone (dead battery, appears as mysterious black mirror)
a wallet with credit cards and driver's license
3 shillings (found in your pocket, origin unknown)

setting.name: London, England, 1547
setting.description: The sprawling, filthy capital of Tudor England under young King Edward VI. Narrow streets wind between timber-framed buildings, while the Thames carries both commerce and sewage. The air reeks of coal smoke, human waste, and unwashed bodies.
setting.tone_notes: Gritty urban squalor mixed with political intrigue. Death lurks around every corner through disease, violence, or accusations of heresy. Dark humor emerges from the absurdity of modern sensibilities clashing with medieval brutality.

acts.title: Strange Awakening
acts.description: Joe must navigate his first hours in Tudor London without being arrested, robbed, or killed. He needs food, shelter, and some understanding of where—and when—he is.
acts.end_condition: Joe finds temporary shelter and realizes he's truly in the 16th century
locked_constraints: Joe cannot reveal his true origins yet
No major authority figures learn of Joe's strangeness
key_beats: Realize the truth about time travel

acts.title: The Prophet's Gambit
acts.description: Joe attempts to use his knowledge of future events to improve his situation, but his meddling attracts dangerous attention from both criminals and authorities.
acts.end_condition: Joe's 'prophecies' put him in serious danger with powerful enemies
locked_constraints: The authorities cannot capture Joe permanently
key_beats: Joe tries to profit from future knowledge
Gains followers who believe he's a prophet
Attracts attention from the wrong people

acts.title: Reckoning
acts.description: Hunted by authorities and criminals alike, Joe must choose between using his dangerous knowledge to escape or accepting his fate in this brutal century.
acts.end_condition: Joe either finds a way back to his time or accepts his new life
locked_constraints: The central mystery must be resolved
key_beats: Desperate escape attempt or last stand

npcs.name: Meg the Tapster
npcs.secret: She's been watering down the ale and skimming profits to feed orphans

npcs.name: Thomas Blackwood
npcs.motivation: Advance his position in court through any means necessary
npcs.secret: He's planning to frame Joe as a French spy to gain favor with the Privy Council

npcs.name: Will the Cutpurse
npcs.secret: He's an informant for the authorities and will betray Joe if the price is right

npcs.name: Brother Francis
npcs.motivation: Determine if Joe is blessed or cursed by God
npcs.secret: He's lost his faith but continues his duties out of fear

factions.name: The Crown
factions.description: Royal authorities and their agents, paranoid about foreign threats and religious heresy
factions.goals: Maintain order and root out enemies of the realm

factions.name: The Southwark Gang
factions.description: Criminal organization controlling the south bank of the Thames
factions.goals: Profit from smuggling, theft, and protection rackets while avoiding the authorities

world_rules: Knowledge of future events is considered witchcraft or foreign espionage
Modern technology appears as mysterious artifacts that arouse suspicion
Class distinctions are rigid and violating them brings severe punishment
Religious orthodoxy is enforced with violence
Life is cheap and death comes easily through disease, violence, or starvation

data.passagePreview: Your skull feels like it's been used as a blacksmith's anvil, and the stench hitting your nostrils i

data.passage:
Your skull feels like it's been used as a blacksmith's anvil, and the stench hitting your nostrils is a symphony of rotting fish, human waste, and something that might charitably be called ale. You crack open your eyes to find yourself sprawled face-down on wet cobblestones, your cheek pressed against what you desperately hope is just muddy water.

The alley stretches before you like a medieval nightmare—timber-framed buildings lean inward overhead, their upper stories nearly touching, blocking out most of the gray morning light. A rat the size of a small dog eyes you with professional interest before scurrying over a pile of refuse that looks suspiciously fresh.

Your clothes feel wrong. The fabric is coarse and scratchy against your skin, nothing like the cotton blend you remember wearing to Murphy's Bar last night. Or was it last night? The memories are fragments: shots of whiskey, someone's birthday, stumbling out into the parking lot...

A cart rumbles past the alley mouth, its wooden wheels clattering over stone, pulled by a horse whose hooves spark against the cobbles. No engine noise. No car horns. Just the distant sounds of voices calling in accents you can't quite place, and somewhere, the tolling of church bells.

You push yourself up on shaking arms, your head spinning. In your pocket, something jangles—coins, but they feel heavier than quarters. Your phone is there too, its familiar weight reassuring until you realize the screen is completely black.

A.text: Examine the strange coins in your pocket more closely
A.consequence: Joe discovers the coins are Tudor-era shillings, providing first concrete evidence of time displacement
A.narration_directive: Focus on Joe's growing realization that these aren't modern coins. Describe the weight, markings, and unfamiliar designs that suggest he's not in 2024 anymore.

B.text: Try to turn on your phone and figure out where you are
B.consequence: The dead phone increases Joe's panic and confusion, but he might be spotted by locals who view the device with suspicion
B.narration_directive: Show Joe's mounting fear as the phone won't respond. Have a passerby glimpse the strange black rectangle and react with wariness or fear.

C.text: Stand up and venture toward the street to get your bearings
C.consequence: Joe gets his first full view of Tudor London street life, confirming his time displacement but remaining relatively unnoticed
C.narration_directive: Reveal the bustling medieval street scene - horse carts, period clothing, timber buildings. Joe realizes the truth but blends into the crowd for now.

D.text: Stay put and try to remember exactly what happened last night
D.consequence: Staying motionless in the alley attracts unwanted attention from cutpurses and vagrants who see an easy mark
D.narration_directive: Have threatening figures approach Joe while he's vulnerable and distracted. Create immediate danger that forces him to act or face robbery/violence.

- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

This is the OPENING PASSAGE. No player choice was made yet. Do not apply any negative status effects or penalties — the player has not made any choices yet. time_elapsed should reflect the scene's timeframe.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
Your skull feels like it's been used as a blacksmith's anvil, and the stench hitting your nostrils is a symphony of rotting fish, human waste, and something that might charitably be called ale. You crack open your eyes to find yourself sprawled face-down on wet cobblestones, your cheek pressed against what you desperately hope is just muddy water.

The alley stretches before you like a medieval nightmare—timber-framed buildings lean inward overhead, their upper stories nearly touching, blocking out most of the gray morning light. A rat the size of a small dog eyes you with professional interest before scurrying over a pile of refuse that looks suspiciously fresh.

Your clothes feel wrong. The fabric is coarse and scratchy against your skin, nothing like the cotton blend you remember wearing to Murphy's Bar last night. Or was it last night? The memories are fragments: shots of whiskey, someone's birthday, stumbling out into the parking lot...

A cart rumbles past the alley mouth, its wooden wheels clattering over stone, pulled by a horse whose hooves spark against the cobbles. No engine noise. No car horns. Just the distant sounds of voices calling in accents you can't quite place, and somewhere, the tolling of church bells.

You push yourself up on shaking arms, your head spinning. In your pocket, something jangles—coins, but they feel heavier than quarters. Your phone is there too, its familiar weight reassuring until you realize the screen is completely black.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Examine the strange coins in your pocket more closely
B: Try to turn on your phone and figure out where you are
C: Stand up and venture toward the street to get your bearings
D: Stay put and try to remember exactly what happened last night

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
Your trembling fingers fumble for the familiar weight of your phone, pulling the sleek black rectangle from your pocket with desperate hope. The screen remains stubbornly dark despite your frantic pressing of buttons. Dead. Completely, utterly dead. The reality crashes over you like a bucket of Thames water—no signal bars, no time display, no GPS to tell you where the hell you are.

A wave of nausea that has nothing to do with your hangover churns in your gut. The cobblestones beneath you are real. The acrid stench of human waste and rotting vegetables is real. The distant clatter of wooden wheels on stone is real.

"Sweet Mary's mercy, what devilry is that?" 

You jerk your head up to find a gap-toothed woman in a grimy brown dress backing away from you, her eyes fixed on your phone with unmistakable terror. Her calloused hands clutch a wicker basket of wilted vegetables as she makes the sign of the cross. "Black magic! Witchcraft!" she hisses, her voice rising to attract unwanted attention.

Other figures begin to emerge from doorways and shadowed alcoves, drawn by her cries. Dirty faces peer at you with mixtures of curiosity and suspicion. Your modern clothes—wrinkled polo shirt and jeans that must look like foreign garments to them—only add to their wariness.

Your head pounds mercilessly as panic sets in.

A.text: Quickly hide the phone and try to calm the frightened woman
A.consequence: Joe manages to defuse the immediate tension and avoid drawing more attention
A.narration_directive: Show Joe successfully calming the woman through humble gestures and hiding the phone. The crowd disperses but remains wary.

B.text: Stand up and walk away before more people gather
B.consequence: Walking away draws more attention and makes Joe look guilty, potentially leading to pursuit
B.narration_directive: Joe's retreat makes him appear suspicious. Some of the gathering crowd begins to follow, muttering about foreign spies or witches.

C.text: Try to explain that it's just a broken mirror or trinket
C.consequence: Attempting to explain the phone as a mirror backfires spectacularly, confirming their suspicions of dark magic
C.narration_directive: Joe's explanation only makes things worse. The crowd becomes hostile, someone shouts for the constables, and Joe faces immediate danger.

D.text: Look around desperately for somewhere to hide or escape
D.consequence: Joe finds a potential escape route but must act quickly before the crowd blocks his path
D.narration_directive: Joe spots a narrow alley or doorway that could provide escape, but the gathering crowd is making it harder to reach safety unnoticed.

- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.

- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:

- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.

PASSAGE:
Your trembling fingers fumble for the familiar weight of your phone, pulling the sleek black rectangle from your pocket with desperate hope. The screen remains stubbornly dark despite your frantic pressing of buttons. Dead. Completely, utterly dead. The reality crashes over you like a bucket of Thames water—no signal bars, no time display, no GPS to tell you where the hell you are.

A wave of nausea that has nothing to do with your hangover churns in your gut. The cobblestones beneath you are real. The acrid stench of human waste and rotting vegetables is real. The distant clatter of wooden wheels on stone is real.

"Sweet Mary's mercy, what devilry is that?" 

You jerk your head up to find a gap-toothed woman in a grimy brown dress backing away from you, her eyes fixed on your phone with unmistakable terror. Her calloused hands clutch a wicker basket of wilted vegetables as she makes the sign of the cross. "Black magic! Witchcraft!" she hisses, her voice rising to attract unwanted attention.

Other figures begin to emerge from doorways and shadowed alcoves, drawn by her cries. Dirty faces peer at you with mixtures of curiosity and suspicion. Your modern clothes—wrinkled polo shirt and jeans that must look like foreign garments to them—only add to their wariness.

Your head pounds mercilessly as panic sets in.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Quickly hide the phone and try to calm the frightened woman
B: Stand up and walk away before more people gather
C: Try to explain that it's just a broken mirror or trinket
D: Look around desperately for somewhere to hide or escape

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: Your words hit the crowd like oil on flames. "A mirror?" the woman shrieks, her voice cracking with

data.passage: Your words hit the crowd like oil on flames. "A mirror?" the woman shrieks, her voice cracking with hysteria. "What manner of mirror shows no reflection? What trinket bears such perfect blackness?" The gathered faces twist with suspicion and fear, and you realize your modern logic has damned you utterly. A burly blacksmith steps forward, hammer still gripped in his soot-stained fist. "No honest Christian carries such devil's glass," he growls. "I've seen scrying bowls in the old country—this reeks of the same dark craft." The crowd presses closer, a wall of unwashed bodies and hostile eyes. Your hangover pounds against your skull like a blacksmith's anvil, and panic claws at your throat as someone bellows, "Fetch the constables! We've caught ourselves a warlock!" Heavy footsteps echo from a nearby street—the unmistakable sound of authority approaching. The phone feels like molten lead in your trembling hands, this cursed artifact that's marked you as surely as if you'd sprouted horns. The crowd's mood shifts from suspicion to ugly certainty, and you catch the glint of steel as someone draws a knife. Time slows as you realize you have perhaps seconds before this turns from accusation to violence.

A.text: Drop the phone and run immediately before the constables arrive
A.consequence: Joe escapes the immediate crowd but loses the phone and gains unwanted attention from authorities
A.narration_directive: Joe drops the phone and flees through the narrow streets, but the constables give chase and his reputation as a warlock begins to spread

B.text: Throw the phone at someone and fight your way through the crowd
B.consequence: Joe injures someone with the phone but gets badly beaten by the crowd before escaping

D.narration_directive: Joe's threat proves his dark nature to the crowd, who attack with deadly force to stop the warlock before he can curse them

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "status_effects": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",

          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "new_pending_consequences": [ { "id": "string", "description": "string", "trigger": "string", "severity": "string", "time_remaining": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 } } ],
    "resolved_consequences": [ "ids of consequences that fired this turn" ],
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|death|hidden_benefit", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option C.
Pre-classified outcome: SEVERE_PENALTY
Pre-determined consequence: "Attempting to explain the phone as a mirror backfires spectacularly, confirming their suspicions of dark magic"
Narration directive: "Joe's explanation only makes things worse. The crowd becomes hostile, someone shouts for the constables, and Joe faces immediate danger."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
Your words hit the crowd like oil on flames. "A mirror?" the woman shrieks, her voice cracking with hysteria. "What manner of mirror shows no reflection? What trinket bears such perfect blackness?" The gathered faces twist with suspicion and fear, and you realize your modern logic has damned you utterly. A burly blacksmith steps forward, hammer still gripped in his soot-stained fist. "No honest Christian carries such devil's glass," he growls. "I've seen scrying bowls in the old country—this reeks of the same dark craft." The crowd presses closer, a wall of unwashed bodies and hostile eyes. Your hangover pounds against your skull like a blacksmith's anvil, and panic claws at your throat as someone bellows, "Fetch the constables! We've caught ourselves a warlock!" Heavy footsteps echo from a nearby street—the unmistakable sound of authority approaching. The phone feels like molten lead in your trembling hands, this cursed artifact that's marked you as surely as if you'd sprouted horns. The crowd's mood shifts from suspicion to ugly certainty, and you catch the glint of steel as someone draws a knife. Time slows as you realize you have perhaps seconds before this turns from accusation to violence.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Drop the phone and run immediately before the constables arrive
B: Throw the phone at someone and fight your way through the crowd
C: Fall to your knees and beg for mercy, claiming you found the cursed object
D: Back against the wall and threaten to curse anyone who comes closer

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: Your knees crack against the filthy cobblestones as you collapse, clutching your throbbing skull wit

data.passage:
Your knees crack against the filthy cobblestones as you collapse, clutching your throbbing skull with both hands. 'Please!' you wail, your voice cracking with genuine terror and hangover-induced nausea. 'I found this cursed thing in the gutter—I swear on Christ's wounds, I know not what evil it holds!' Tears stream down your face, mixing desperation with the bile rising in your throat.

The crowd's bloodthirsty murmur falters. A weathered woman pushes forward, her voice cutting through the mob's uncertainty: 'Look at the poor wretch—he's more terrified than we are!' She turns to face the others, hands on her hips. 'What warlock would cower like a beaten dog?'

Doubt ripples through the gathering as constables' boots echo closer down the narrow street. The man with the knife wavers, his grip loosening. 'But the devil's mirror—'

'Could've been dropped by anyone,' the woman snaps. 'French spies, mayhap, or some merchant trafficking in foreign devilry.'

Will the Cutpurse materializes beside you, his scarred face a mask of concern. 'Come, friend,' he whispers urgently, 'while they're arguing amongst themselves.' He helps you to your feet as the crowd fragments into heated debate. The constables are perhaps twenty paces away, their authority cutting through the morning mist like a blade.

A.text: Follow Will through the crowd while everyone is distracted
A.consequence: Will leads Joe through back alleys to temporary safety, avoiding the constables
A.narration_directive: Will proves knowledgeable about escape routes, leading Joe away from danger through narrow passages the authorities don't know

B.text: Grab your phone and run in the opposite direction from the constables
B.consequence: Joe retrieves his phone but draws renewed attention, forcing a dangerous chase
B.narration_directive: Grabbing the phone reignites suspicions and Joe must flee with constables and some crowd members in pursuit

C.text: Stay and try to convince the sympathetic woman to hide you
C.consequence: The woman tries to help but constables arrive and begin questioning everyone present
C.narration_directive: Goodwife Martha attempts to hide Joe but the constables' arrival forces an uncomfortable interrogation

D.text: Pretend to faint to buy more time and avoid the approaching constables
D.consequence: Feigning unconsciousness backfires when constables decide to arrest the 'warlock' for questioning
D.narration_directive: The fake faint convinces the constables Joe is dangerous and they move to restrain him while he's 'helpless'

{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "status_effects": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "new_pending_consequences": [ { "id": "string", "description": "string", "trigger": "string", "severity": "string", "time_remaining": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 } } ],
    "resolved_consequences": [ "ids of consequences that fired this turn" ],
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|death|hidden_benefit", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option C.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Joe's desperate plea convinces some in the crowd he's a victim, creating confusion that allows escape"
Narration directive: "Joe's convincing performance as an innocent victim creates doubt in the crowd, and a sympathetic voice speaks up, giving him a chance to slip away"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
Your knees crack against the filthy cobblestones as you collapse, clutching your throbbing skull with both hands. 'Please!' you wail, your voice cracking with genuine terror and hangover-induced nausea. 'I found this cursed thing in the gutter—I swear on Christ's wounds, I know not what evil it holds!' Tears stream down your face, mixing desperation with the bile rising in your throat.

The crowd's bloodthirsty murmur falters. A weathered woman pushes forward, her voice cutting through the mob's uncertainty: 'Look at the poor wretch—he's more terrified than we are!' She turns to face the others, hands on her hips. 'What warlock would cower like a beaten dog?'

Doubt ripples through the gathering as constables' boots echo closer down the narrow street. The man with the knife wavers, his grip loosening. 'But the devil's mirror—'

'Could've been dropped by anyone,' the woman snaps. 'French spies, mayhap, or some merchant trafficking in foreign devilry.'

Will the Cutpurse materializes beside you, his scarred face a mask of concern. 'Come, friend,' he whispers urgently, 'while they're arguing amongst themselves.' He helps you to your feet as the crowd fragments into heated debate. The constables are perhaps twenty paces away, their authority cutting through the morning mist like a blade.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Follow Will through the crowd while everyone is distracted
B: Grab your phone and run in the opposite direction from the constables
C: Stay and try to convince the sympathetic woman to hide you
D: Pretend to faint to buy more time and avoid the approaching constables

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: Will's calloused hand grips your arm with surprising strength as he pulls you through the dispersing

data.passage:
Will's calloused hand grips your arm with surprising strength as he pulls you through the dispersing crowd. "This way, prophet," he whispers, the title dripping with equal parts mockery and opportunity. Your splitting head pounds with each hurried step as he guides you between arguing onlookers, past Goodwife Martha who catches your eye with a knowing nod.

The cutpurse knows these streets like a spider knows its web. He leads you down a narrow passage between two timber-framed buildings, the walls so close you could touch both sides with outstretched arms. The stench of rotting vegetables and human waste assaults your nostrils, but it's preferable to a constable's cudgel. Behind you, authoritative voices boom as the law arrives to question witnesses about the "foreign warlock."

"Lucky for you I was there," Will mutters, navigating a maze of interconnected alleys that twist like a drunken man's path home. "Unlucky for you, salvation ain't free." His eyes glitter with avarice as he stops beside a weathered door marked only by a crude carving of a fish. "Safe house. But first, we need to discuss terms. That little trinket of yours—the black mirror—it's worth more than you know in the right hands."

Your hangover throbs as reality settles in: you've escaped immediate danger, but you're far from free.

A.text: Agree to Will's terms and enter the safe house
A.consequence: Joe gains temporary shelter but loses his phone to Will, who may use it for his own schemes
A.narration_directive: Joe reluctantly hands over the phone and enters the safe house, but Will's intentions with the device remain unclear and potentially dangerous

B.text: Refuse to give up your phone and try to find shelter elsewhere
B.consequence: Joe refuses Will's help and must find shelter alone while authorities actively search for him
B.narration_directive: Joe strikes out on his own in unfamiliar Tudor streets with constables hunting for the 'warlock' and no knowledge of safe places to hide

C.text: Demand to know more about Will and his connections before deciding
C.consequence: Will reveals more about his criminal connections and the value of Joe's 'trinket' in the underworld
C.narration_directive: Will explains his role in the Southwark Gang and how Joe's mysterious device could be valuable to certain collectors of curiosities

D.text: Offer Will something else in exchange for his help
D.consequence: Joe offers his modern wallet/cards as payment, which Will finds intriguing but insufficient
D.narration_directive: Will examines Joe's strange cards and papers with growing suspicion about Joe's true origins, demanding both the wallet and the phone

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

PASSAGE:
Will's calloused hand grips your arm with surprising strength as he pulls you through the dispersing crowd. "This way, prophet," he whispers, the title dripping with equal parts mockery and opportunity. Your splitting head pounds with each hurried step as he guides you between arguing onlookers, past Goodwife Martha who catches your eye with a knowing nod.

The cutpurse knows these streets like a spider knows its web. He leads you down a narrow passage between two timber-framed buildings, the walls so close you could touch both sides with outstretched arms. The stench of rotting vegetables and human waste assaults your nostrils, but it's preferable to a constable's cudgel. Behind you, authoritative voices boom as the law arrives to question witnesses about the "foreign warlock."

"Lucky for you I was there," Will mutters, navigating a maze of interconnected alleys that twist like a drunken man's path home. "Unlucky for you, salvation ain't free." His eyes glitter with avarice as he stops beside a weathered door marked only by a crude carving of a fish. "Safe house. But first, we need to discuss terms. That little trinket of yours—the black mirror—it's worth more than you know in the right hands."

Your hangover throbs as reality settles in: you've escaped immediate danger, but you're far from free.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Agree to Will's terms and enter the safe house
B: Refuse to give up your phone and try to find shelter elsewhere
C: Demand to know more about Will and his connections before deciding
D: Offer Will something else in exchange for his help

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You plant your feet firmly on the warped floorboards, ignoring the pounding in your skull. "Not so fast, Will. Before I hand over anything, I want to know exactly who you are and what kind of 'connections' you're talking about."

Will's grin falters slightly, but he seems to appreciate your caution. He leans against the grimy wall, arms crossed. "Fair enough, mate. Name's Will Harrow, though most know me as Will the Cutpurse. I run with the Southwark boys—we control most everything south of the Thames. Smuggling, protection, information... the usual trades."

He eyes your phone hungrily. "That black mirror of yours? I've got contacts who collect strange trinkets—wealthy merchants, foreign ambassadors, even some courtiers who fancy themselves scholars of mysteries. They pay handsomely for curiosities, especially ones that make crowds think of witchcraft."

Outside, you hear distant shouts and the clatter of boots on cobblestones. The constables haven't given up their search. Will notices your tension. "Look, Joe, I can keep you safe from the law, but my protection ain't free. That device could fetch enough coin to buy you a new identity, proper clothes, maybe even passage out of London if things get too hot."

The hangover throbs behind your eyes as you weigh your options. Trust a criminal with your only link to the modern world, or face the Tudor authorities alone?

A.narration_directive: Will takes the phone and explains the gang's protection network, but Joe realizes he's now indebted to criminals

B.text: Refuse the deal and try to slip away while he's distracted

C.text: Propose a partnership instead—you'll work together but keep the phone

D.narration_directive: Will agrees but warns the collector is dangerous, setting up a risky encounter with unknown consequences

{

          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "new_pending_consequences": [ { "id": "string", "description": "string", "trigger": "string", "severity": "string", "time_remaining": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 } } ],
    "resolved_consequences": [ "ids of consequences that fired this turn" ],
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|death|hidden_benefit", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option C.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Will reveals more about his criminal connections and the value of Joe's 'trinket' in the underworld"
Narration directive: "Will explains his role in the Southwark Gang and how Joe's mysterious device could be valuable to certain collectors of curiosities"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You plant your feet firmly on the warped floorboards, ignoring the pounding in your skull. "Not so fast, Will. Before I hand over anything, I want to know exactly who you are and what kind of 'connections' you're talking about."

Will's grin falters slightly, but he seems to appreciate your caution. He leans against the grimy wall, arms crossed. "Fair enough, mate. Name's Will Harrow, though most know me as Will the Cutpurse. I run with the Southwark boys—we control most everything south of the Thames. Smuggling, protection, information... the usual trades."

He eyes your phone hungrily. "That black mirror of yours? I've got contacts who collect strange trinkets—wealthy merchants, foreign ambassadors, even some courtiers who fancy themselves scholars of mysteries. They pay handsomely for curiosities, especially ones that make crowds think of witchcraft."

Outside, you hear distant shouts and the clatter of boots on cobblestones. The constables haven't given up their search. Will notices your tension. "Look, Joe, I can keep you safe from the law, but my protection ain't free. That device could fetch enough coin to buy you a new identity, proper clothes, maybe even passage out of London if things get too hot."

The hangover throbs behind your eyes as you weigh your options. Trust a criminal with your only link to the modern world, or face the Tudor authorities alone?

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Hand over the phone and accept Will's protection
B: Refuse the deal and try to slip away while he's distracted
C: Propose a partnership instead—you'll work together but keep the phone
D: Ask Will to arrange a meeting with one of these mysterious collectors first

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
Will's eyes narrow as he considers your proposal, his fingers drumming against the grimy brick wall. The distant shouts of constables echo through the maze of alleys, but they seem to be moving away from your position. Your head throbs with each heartbeat, the hangover making it difficult to focus on his calculating expression.

"A partnership, eh?" Will grins, revealing yellowed teeth. "I like a man with spine. Keep your cursed mirror—I've seen what happens when folks get too greedy with strange magicks." He spits into the muck at his feet. "But I want half of whatever we make from your... talents."

He leads you deeper into a warren of ramshackle buildings, the timber frames sagging under centuries of London's weight. "The Southwark boys will want to meet you proper-like. They've got safe houses where even the King's men fear to tread." Will pauses at a low doorway marked only by a crude chalk symbol. "But first, you need to look less like a foreign devil. These clothes of yours..." He shakes his head. "They scream 'burn me at the stake.'"

The reality hits you like a physical blow—you're truly trapped in the sixteenth century, dependent on criminals for survival. The smartphone in your pocket feels both like salvation and damnation.

A.text: Accept Will's terms and enter the safe house
A.consequence: Joe enters the safe house and gains temporary sanctuary with the gang
A.narration_directive: Will leads Joe into the safe house where he meets other gang members who are curious but welcoming

B.text: Demand better terms before agreeing to meet his gang
B.consequence: Will becomes irritated by Joe's demands, potentially souring the partnership before it begins
B.narration_directive: Will's expression hardens as Joe pushes for better terms, testing the limits of their new alliance

C.text: Ask Will to find you period-appropriate clothing first
C.consequence: Will agrees to help Joe blend in, strengthening their partnership and reducing suspicion
C.narration_directive: Will nods approvingly at Joe's practical thinking and offers to procure suitable Tudor clothing

D.text: Insist on understanding exactly what his gang expects from you
D.consequence: Joe's questions reveal his complete ignorance of criminal life, making Will suspicious of his true nature
D.narration_directive: Will's eyes narrow dangerously as Joe's questions expose how little he understands about the world he's entered

  },

    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

PASSAGE:
Will's eyes narrow as he considers your proposal, his fingers drumming against the grimy brick wall. The distant shouts of constables echo through the maze of alleys, but they seem to be moving away from your position. Your head throbs with each heartbeat, the hangover making it difficult to focus on his calculating expression.

"A partnership, eh?" Will grins, revealing yellowed teeth. "I like a man with spine. Keep your cursed mirror—I've seen what happens when folks get too greedy with strange magicks." He spits into the muck at his feet. "But I want half of whatever we make from your... talents."

He leads you deeper into a warren of ramshackle buildings, the timber frames sagging under centuries of London's weight. "The Southwark boys will want to meet you proper-like. They've got safe houses where even the King's men fear to tread." Will pauses at a low doorway marked only by a crude chalk symbol. "But first, you need to look less like a foreign devil. These clothes of yours..." He shakes his head. "They scream 'burn me at the stake.'"

The reality hits you like a physical blow—you're truly trapped in the sixteenth century, dependent on criminals for survival. The smartphone in your pocket feels both like salvation and damnation.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Accept Will's terms and enter the safe house
B: Demand better terms before agreeing to meet his gang
C: Ask Will to find you period-appropriate clothing first
D: Insist on understanding exactly what his gang expects from you

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.
