PASSAGE:
Will's face hardens as you grab his arm, desperation bleeding through your voice. "We have to leave London. Now. Before they organize properly." The distant sound of Mother Blacktooth's screams carries on the night wind, each shriek another nail in your coffin.

"Aye, I figured you'd come to that," Will mutters, spitting into the gutter. "Problem is, getting out ain't like walking through a door. The gang controls the river crossings, the main roads... everything worth using." He glances toward the Thames, where torchlight flickers on the water. "And they don't do charity work."

Your stomach drops. "What kind of price?"

"Big Tom wants proof of loyalty before he risks his smuggling routes." Will's voice drops to a whisper. "There's a merchant house on Pudding Lane—rivals moving into gang territory. He wants it burned. Tonight. With you holding the torch." The implications hit you like a physical blow. Arson. Murder, potentially. Your modern sensibilities recoil, but another scream from the witch hunter's victim reminds you of the alternative.

"Do that, and Tom'll have a fast boat waiting at Rotherhithe. Refuse..." Will shrugs. "Well, you can try swimming the Thames in those fancy clothes."

The city's bells toll three in the morning. Dawn—and the manhunt—draws closer with each passing minute.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Agree to burn the merchant house to secure safe passage out of London
B: Refuse the arson and attempt to find another way out of the city
C: Try to negotiate a different price with the Southwark Gang
D: Suggest using your 'prophetic' knowledge to offer the gang something more valuable than arson

data.passage:
Will's eyes narrow as you mention prophetic knowledge, his scarred hand pausing mid-gesture. "Prophecies, eh?" He leans against the grimy warehouse wall, studying you with newfound interest. "What sort of... insights... might you be offering?"

Your heart pounds as precious minutes tick away. Through the broken window, you can hear distant shouts—the witch hunters are still scouring the streets, and Mother Blacktooth's interrogation grows closer to revealing everything. The gang members behind Will shift restlessly, their weapons catching moonlight.

"The merchants' guild," you say carefully, "I know which ships will arrive late, which cargo will spoil. Information worth more than any burned warehouse." It's a desperate gambit—your scattered memories of Tudor history mixed with pure invention.

Will's scarred face breaks into a predatory grin. "Now that's thinking like a proper criminal." He exchanges glances with his men. "But prophecies are tricky things, foreign friend. Easy to claim, harder to prove. And if you're wrong..." He draws a finger across his throat.

The distant shouts grow closer. Dawn approaches, bringing with it every constable in London hunting for the foreign warlock. Your false Flemish identity hangs by a thread, and your mysterious device has marked you for death. But Will's interest offers a sliver of hope—if you can convince him your knowledge is valuable enough to risk his gang's protection on.

"Prove it," he says simply. "Give me something I can verify tonight, or it's back to the arson plan."

A.text: Claim knowledge of a merchant ship arriving early at London Bridge with valuable cargo
A.consequence: Specific claim about merchant ship creates verifiable timeline but risks exposure if wrong
A.narration_directive: Will sends men to verify the ship claim while Joe waits anxiously for results

B.text: Warn about a constable patrol that will pass through Southwark within the hour
B.consequence: Warning about patrol demonstrates immediate value and builds trust with gang
B.narration_directive: Will's men confirm the patrol, proving Joe's worth and securing gang protection

C.text: Reveal information about Thomas Blackwood's movements and vulnerabilities
C.consequence: Revealing Blackwood information exposes Joe's dangerous knowledge and creates new enemies
C.narration_directive: Will realizes Joe knows too much about court politics, making him a liability

D.text: Admit you need time to 'commune with spirits' for accurate prophecies
D.consequence: Stalling tactic buys time but increases suspicion about Joe's true abilities
D.narration_directive: Will grows impatient with delays as time runs out and authorities close in

- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

Pre-determined consequence: "Joe's future knowledge intrigues the gang enough to consider alternative payment"
Narration directive: "Will becomes interested in Joe's prophetic abilities as potential gang asset"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
Will's eyes narrow as you mention prophetic knowledge, his scarred hand pausing mid-gesture. "Prophecies, eh?" He leans against the grimy warehouse wall, studying you with newfound interest. "What sort of... insights... might you be offering?"

Your heart pounds as precious minutes tick away. Through the broken window, you can hear distant shouts—the witch hunters are still scouring the streets, and Mother Blacktooth's interrogation grows closer to revealing everything. The gang members behind Will shift restlessly, their weapons catching moonlight.

"The merchants' guild," you say carefully, "I know which ships will arrive late, which cargo will spoil. Information worth more than any burned warehouse." It's a desperate gambit—your scattered memories of Tudor history mixed with pure invention.

Will's scarred face breaks into a predatory grin. "Now that's thinking like a proper criminal." He exchanges glances with his men. "But prophecies are tricky things, foreign friend. Easy to claim, harder to prove. And if you're wrong..." He draws a finger across his throat.

The distant shouts grow closer. Dawn approaches, bringing with it every constable in London hunting for the foreign warlock. Your false Flemish identity hangs by a thread, and your mysterious device has marked you for death. But Will's interest offers a sliver of hope—if you can convince him your knowledge is valuable enough to risk his gang's protection on.

"Prove it," he says simply. "Give me something I can verify tonight, or it's back to the arson plan."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Claim knowledge of a merchant ship arriving early at London Bridge with valuable cargo
B: Warn about a constable patrol that will pass through Southwark within the hour
C: Reveal information about Thomas Blackwood's movements and vulnerabilities
D: Admit you need time to 'commune with spirits' for accurate prophecies

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You lean forward, your voice urgent in the flickering candlelight. "There's a constable patrol—six men with torches—that will come through Southwark in forty minutes. They'll take the main road past the docks, checking every alehouse for smugglers."

Will's scarred face hardens as he studies you. "How could you possibly—" He stops mid-sentence, then barks an order to a gap-toothed man by the door. "Fetch Knife-ear Tom. Tell him to watch the Southwark road."

Minutes crawl by like hours as sweat beads on your forehead. The cramped room reeks of unwashed bodies and stale ale, but you can barely breathe thinking about Mother Blacktooth spilling everything to her captors. Every shadow beyond the shutters could be a constable's torch.

Then Tom bursts through the door, eyes wide. "Will! Six constables coming down the main road, just like the prophet said! They're checking the Anchor and Crown right now!"

The room erupts in impressed murmurs. Will's expression transforms from suspicion to calculation as he waves the men to silence. "Well, Joe," he says slowly, "seems your gift is real enough. The boys'll remember this—you just saved us from a night in the stocks." His grin turns predatory. "Which means we can discuss alternatives to that little fire I mentioned. Tell me more about what's coming."

A.text: Predict that Thomas Blackwood will arrive at dawn with soldiers to search for foreign spies
A.consequence: Accurate but dangerous prediction draws Blackwood's attention directly to Joe
A.narration_directive: Will is impressed but warns this prediction will make Blackwood hunt Joe personally

B.text: Warn that a fire will break out in the grain warehouses before sunrise, creating chaos you could use to escape
B.consequence: Creates useful chaos for escape while maintaining prophetic credibility
B.narration_directive: Will sees the strategic value and begins planning an escape using the fire as cover

C.text: Reveal that the witch hunters will torture Mother Blacktooth until she gives them your exact description
C.consequence: Reveals too much knowledge about current events, making Will suspicious of Joe's true nature
C.narration_directive: Will becomes deeply suspicious about how Joe knows such specific details about ongoing interrogations

D.text: Claim you see a safe passage opening through the sewers beneath London Bridge within the hour
D.consequence: Sewer route exists but is extremely dangerous and may not lead to true escape
D.narration_directive: Will knows the sewers but warns they're deadly - full of disease, cave-ins, and worse things

DIFFICULTY: Hard

- Consequence severity: severe
- Game over allowed: true
- Hint transparency: subtle
- NPC forgiveness: low
- Allow lethal effects: true
- Max effect severity: 1
- Healing speed: realistic

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "status_effects": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "new_pending_consequences": [ { "id": "string", "description": "string", "trigger": "string", "severity": "string", "time_remaining": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 } } ],
    "resolved_consequences": [ "ids of consequences that fired this turn" ],
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|death|hidden_benefit", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option B.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Warning about patrol demonstrates immediate value and builds trust with gang"
Narration directive: "Will's men confirm the patrol, proving Joe's worth and securing gang protection"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You lean forward, your voice urgent in the flickering candlelight. "There's a constable patrol—six men with torches—that will come through Southwark in forty minutes. They'll take the main road past the docks, checking every alehouse for smugglers."

Will's scarred face hardens as he studies you. "How could you possibly—" He stops mid-sentence, then barks an order to a gap-toothed man by the door. "Fetch Knife-ear Tom. Tell him to watch the Southwark road."

Minutes crawl by like hours as sweat beads on your forehead. The cramped room reeks of unwashed bodies and stale ale, but you can barely breathe thinking about Mother Blacktooth spilling everything to her captors. Every shadow beyond the shutters could be a constable's torch.

Then Tom bursts through the door, eyes wide. "Will! Six constables coming down the main road, just like the prophet said! They're checking the Anchor and Crown right now!"

The room erupts in impressed murmurs. Will's expression transforms from suspicion to calculation as he waves the men to silence. "Well, Joe," he says slowly, "seems your gift is real enough. The boys'll remember this—you just saved us from a night in the stocks." His grin turns predatory. "Which means we can discuss alternatives to that little fire I mentioned. Tell me more about what's coming."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Predict that Thomas Blackwood will arrive at dawn with soldiers to search for foreign spies
B: Warn that a fire will break out in the grain warehouses before sunrise, creating chaos you could use to escape
C: Reveal that the witch hunters will torture Mother Blacktooth until she gives them your exact description
D: Claim you see a safe passage opening through the sewers beneath London Bridge within the hour

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: Will's eyes light up with criminal cunning as you describe the warehouse fires. "Bloody brilliant,"

data.passage:
Will's eyes light up with criminal cunning as you describe the warehouse fires. "Bloody brilliant," he whispers, leaning closer in the flickering torchlight. "Half the Watch will rush to fight the blaze, and in all that smoke and confusion..." He trails off, already calculating angles and escape routes. The other gang members exchange knowing glances—they've used similar chaos before.

"But here's the rub," Will continues, his voice dropping to barely audible. "If you're wrong about this fire, we're all dancing on Tyburn Hill come morning. Mother Blacktooth's singing every song she knows to those witch hunters, and sunrise brings every constable in London hunting your foreign hide." His scarred hand grips your shoulder. "So I need details, Prophet. When exactly? Which warehouses? How does it start?"

The urgency in his voice matches the knot in your stomach. Dawn creeps closer with each heartbeat, and somewhere across the city, torturers are extracting every detail about the 'demon's mirror' from a mad woman's lips. The gang waits for your answer, their faith in your supernatural knowledge balanced on a knife's edge. Get this wrong, and you'll face the manhunt alone. Get it right, and you might just live to see another day—if you can stomach the consequences of the chaos you're about to unleash.

A.text: Claim the fire starts from an overturned lantern in the eastern grain stores at exactly 5 AM, giving precise timing
A.consequence: Specific timing creates high expectations but allows precise planning - failure means immediate exposure
A.narration_directive: Will begins detailed escape preparations based on the exact timing, but warns of deadly consequences if Joe is wrong

B.text: Describe multiple fires breaking out across different warehouses simultaneously, suggesting arson rather than accident
B.consequence: Suggesting coordinated arson makes Joe complicit in mass destruction and potential murder
B.narration_directive: Will realizes Joe is describing deliberate arson and questions whether Joe is actually planning to cause the fires himself

C.text: Warn that the fires will spread to nearby houses unless the Watch acts quickly, adding urgency to your prediction
C.consequence: Adding urgency about civilian safety maintains moral credibility while providing tactical value
C.narration_directive: Will appreciates both the tactical information and Joe's concern for innocents, solidifying gang trust

D.text: Admit you can't see the exact details in your 'visions,' but insist the chaos will definitely occur before dawn
D.consequence: Vague predictions maintain mystique but reduce tactical value - gang may demand more concrete proof
D.narration_directive: Will accepts the limitation but demands Joe prove his worth through other means before committing to the escape plan

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
Will's eyes light up with criminal cunning as you describe the warehouse fires. "Bloody brilliant," he whispers, leaning closer in the flickering torchlight. "Half the Watch will rush to fight the blaze, and in all that smoke and confusion..." He trails off, already calculating angles and escape routes. The other gang members exchange knowing glances—they've used similar chaos before.

"But here's the rub," Will continues, his voice dropping to barely audible. "If you're wrong about this fire, we're all dancing on Tyburn Hill come morning. Mother Blacktooth's singing every song she knows to those witch hunters, and sunrise brings every constable in London hunting your foreign hide." His scarred hand grips your shoulder. "So I need details, Prophet. When exactly? Which warehouses? How does it start?"

The urgency in his voice matches the knot in your stomach. Dawn creeps closer with each heartbeat, and somewhere across the city, torturers are extracting every detail about the 'demon's mirror' from a mad woman's lips. The gang waits for your answer, their faith in your supernatural knowledge balanced on a knife's edge. Get this wrong, and you'll face the manhunt alone. Get it right, and you might just live to see another day—if you can stomach the consequences of the chaos you're about to unleash.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Claim the fire starts from an overturned lantern in the eastern grain stores at exactly 5 AM, giving precise timing
B: Describe multiple fires breaking out across different warehouses simultaneously, suggesting arson rather than accident
C: Warn that the fires will spread to nearby houses unless the Watch acts quickly, adding urgency to your prediction
D: Admit you can't see the exact details in your 'visions,' but insist the chaos will definitely occur before dawn

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: Will's eyes sharpen as you speak, and several gang members lean closer. "Aye, that's the kind of det

data.passage: Will's eyes sharpen as you speak, and several gang members lean closer. "Aye, that's the kind of detail that saves lives," he says approvingly. "Most fortune-tellers spout vague nonsense about death and glory. But you're thinking like someone who's seen real fires spread through cramped streets." He exchanges meaningful glances with his lieutenants. "The Watch will have to choose between chasing one foreign warlock or saving half of Cheapside from burning. Creates proper chaos for our purposes, and keeps the blood off our hands." The other gang members nod grimly—even criminals have neighbors they'd rather not see roasted alive. But as Will speaks, you catch the distant sound of church bells tolling the hour. Four-fifteen in the morning. Dawn is less than two hours away, and with it, the city-wide manhunt that will make every street a death trap. Mother Blacktooth's screams have likely already painted you as a demon-conjuring warlock in vivid detail. Will notices your tension. "Time's running short, prophet. The lads are ready to move, but we need more than fire predictions to get you past the river gates. The guards there know every face that tries to flee London. What else can those visions of yours tell us about the roads ahead?"

A.text: Predict which river guards will be drunk or asleep, making escape easier
A.consequence: Accurate guard prediction helps escape but risks exposure if guards change shifts unexpectedly
A.narration_directive: Joe provides specific guard details that prove accurate but create vulnerability if timing goes wrong

B.text: Warn of Crown agents waiting on the main roads out of London
B.consequence: Warning about Crown agents provides valuable intelligence without requiring specific details
B.narration_directive: Will appreciates the strategic warning and adjusts escape plans accordingly

C.text: Describe a merchant caravan that will provide perfect cover for escape
C.consequence: Merchant caravan prediction offers perfect cover but requires precise timing that could fail catastrophically
C.narration_directive: Joe describes a caravan that provides ideal cover but creates dependency on exact timing

D.text: Admit your visions are limited and ask Will to trust your other skills instead
D.consequence: Admitting limitations destroys prophet credibility and forces gang to demand the original arson plan
D.narration_directive: Will's trust evaporates and he reverts to demanding Joe burn the merchant house as originally planned

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

PASSAGE:
Will's eyes sharpen as you speak, and several gang members lean closer. "Aye, that's the kind of detail that saves lives," he says approvingly. "Most fortune-tellers spout vague nonsense about death and glory. But you're thinking like someone who's seen real fires spread through cramped streets." He exchanges meaningful glances with his lieutenants. "The Watch will have to choose between chasing one foreign warlock or saving half of Cheapside from burning. Creates proper chaos for our purposes, and keeps the blood off our hands." The other gang members nod grimly—even criminals have neighbors they'd rather not see roasted alive. But as Will speaks, you catch the distant sound of church bells tolling the hour. Four-fifteen in the morning. Dawn is less than two hours away, and with it, the city-wide manhunt that will make every street a death trap. Mother Blacktooth's screams have likely already painted you as a demon-conjuring warlock in vivid detail. Will notices your tension. "Time's running short, prophet. The lads are ready to move, but we need more than fire predictions to get you past the river gates. The guards there know every face that tries to flee London. What else can those visions of yours tell us about the roads ahead?"

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Predict which river guards will be drunk or asleep, making escape easier
B: Warn of Crown agents waiting on the main roads out of London
C: Describe a merchant caravan that will provide perfect cover for escape
D: Admit your visions are limited and ask Will to trust your other skills instead

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: Will's eyes sharpen as you speak, his weathered face creasing with grim understanding. "Crown agents

data.passage:
Will's eyes sharpen as you speak, his weathered face creasing with grim understanding. "Crown agents on the roads," he mutters, spitting into the muddy water. "Aye, that makes sense. They'd block the main arteries first, catch anyone fleeing the city like fish in a barrel." He adjusts his grip on the oars, steering the small boat toward a cluster of moored barges near the opposite bank. "Good thing we're taking the river then, but even that's not simple."

The Thames flows dark and sluggish around you, carrying the stench of sewage and rotting fish. Dawn creeps closer—you can see the faintest gray lightening the eastern sky beyond the bridge. Will follows your gaze and his jaw tightens. "Sunrise brings the river guards to full strength. They'll be checking every vessel, every face." He nods toward a stone gatehouse rising from the water ahead, torchlight flickering in its windows. "That's our problem right there—London Bridge's river gate. Only way through is past those guards, and they'll be looking for anyone suspicious."

The boat rocks gently as Will slows his rowing. Behind you, the city's bells begin to toll the early hour. Your time is nearly up, and this cramped wooden vessel suddenly feels like a floating coffin. Will stares at you expectantly, waiting for another miracle from his prophet.

A.text: Predict which guard will be drunk and inattentive at the gate
A.consequence: Specific guard prediction creates opportunity but risks exposure if wrong
A.narration_directive: Joe makes a specific prediction about guard behavior that Will can use tactically

B.text: Warn of a merchant vessel that will cause a distraction
B.consequence: Merchant vessel distraction provides cover without requiring specific details
B.narration_directive: Joe's prediction about a merchant distraction gives Will a clear tactical advantage

C.text: Tell Will you've given him enough—demand he honor his word and get you through
C.consequence: Will's trust breaks - he threatens to turn Joe over to authorities unless demands are met
C.narration_directive: Will's anger at Joe's refusal creates immediate danger and ultimatum

D.text: Admit you have no more visions and throw yourself on his mercy
D.consequence: Will abandons Joe at the river gate where dawn patrol captures him
D.narration_directive: Will's disappointment leads to immediate abandonment as guards approach

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:

- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

PASSAGE:
Will's eyes sharpen as you speak, his weathered face creasing with grim understanding. "Crown agents on the roads," he mutters, spitting into the muddy water. "Aye, that makes sense. They'd block the main arteries first, catch anyone fleeing the city like fish in a barrel." He adjusts his grip on the oars, steering the small boat toward a cluster of moored barges near the opposite bank. "Good thing we're taking the river then, but even that's not simple."

The Thames flows dark and sluggish around you, carrying the stench of sewage and rotting fish. Dawn creeps closer—you can see the faintest gray lightening the eastern sky beyond the bridge. Will follows your gaze and his jaw tightens. "Sunrise brings the river guards to full strength. They'll be checking every vessel, every face." He nods toward a stone gatehouse rising from the water ahead, torchlight flickering in its windows. "That's our problem right there—London Bridge's river gate. Only way through is past those guards, and they'll be looking for anyone suspicious."

The boat rocks gently as Will slows his rowing. Behind you, the city's bells begin to toll the early hour. Your time is nearly up, and this cramped wooden vessel suddenly feels like a floating coffin. Will stares at you expectantly, waiting for another miracle from his prophet.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Predict which guard will be drunk and inattentive at the gate
B: Warn of a merchant vessel that will cause a distraction
C: Tell Will you've given him enough—demand he honor his word and get you through
D: Admit you have no more visions and throw yourself on his mercy

2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes

data.passagePreview: "There's a grain barge coming downriver," you whisper urgently, pointing toward the Thames bend. "He

data.passage:
"There's a grain barge coming downriver," you whisper urgently, pointing toward the Thames bend. "Heavy-laden, riding low. The crew's drunk from celebrating a profitable run, and they'll ram straight into the dock pilings just as the guards change shift."

Will's eyes narrow, studying the dark water. "When?"

"Minutes. Maybe less." Your voice carries the weight of certainty that's served you well tonight. "The crash will draw every guard to the waterfront. Cargo spilling, crew screaming, dock workers scrambling to salvage what they can. Pure chaos."

As if summoned by your words, the silhouette of a heavy barge emerges from the morning mist, listing slightly as drunken voices carry across the water. Will grins like a wolf scenting blood.

"Perfect." He adjusts his grip on the stolen oars. "When that barge hits, we row hard for the opposite bank. The guards will be too busy fishing grain sacks and sailors from the Thames to notice two more shadows on the water."

The first pale fingers of dawn stretch across London's rooftops behind you. Every second counts now. Mother Blacktooth's confession has bought you this narrow window, but sunrise will bring the full weight of the Crown's manhunt crashing down. One way or another, your fate in Tudor England will be decided in the next few minutes.

The barge lurches closer to the dock pilings, disaster imminent.

A.text: Wait for the crash, then row immediately for the far shore
A.consequence: Clean escape across the Thames using the distraction as planned
A.narration_directive: Joe and Will successfully escape London using the barge crash distraction, reaching safety on the far shore

B.text: Use the distraction to steal a faster boat from the dock
B.consequence: Boat theft succeeds but draws attention from dock workers who survive to identify them
B.narration_directive: The faster boat provides better escape but creates witnesses who can describe Joe to authorities

C.text: Help rescue the drowning sailors to blend in with the crowd
C.consequence: Rescue attempt exposes Joe to surviving crew who recognize his foreign nature and strange knowledge
C.narration_directive: Joe's heroic act backfires as the rescued sailors become dangerous witnesses to his prophetic abilities

D.text: Abandon the river plan and flee on foot through the chaos
D.consequence: Abandoning the river plan traps Joe in London as the manhunt begins at sunrise
D.narration_directive: Without the river escape, Joe is caught in the city-wide manhunt and captured by witch hunters

    "resolved_consequences": [ "ids of consequences that fired this turn" ],
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|death|hidden_benefit", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.

PASSAGE:
"There's a grain barge coming downriver," you whisper urgently, pointing toward the Thames bend. "Heavy-laden, riding low. The crew's drunk from celebrating a profitable run, and they'll ram straight into the dock pilings just as the guards change shift."

Will's eyes narrow, studying the dark water. "When?"

"Minutes. Maybe less." Your voice carries the weight of certainty that's served you well tonight. "The crash will draw every guard to the waterfront. Cargo spilling, crew screaming, dock workers scrambling to salvage what they can. Pure chaos."

As if summoned by your words, the silhouette of a heavy barge emerges from the morning mist, listing slightly as drunken voices carry across the water. Will grins like a wolf scenting blood.

"Perfect." He adjusts his grip on the stolen oars. "When that barge hits, we row hard for the opposite bank. The guards will be too busy fishing grain sacks and sailors from the Thames to notice two more shadows on the water."

The first pale fingers of dawn stretch across London's rooftops behind you. Every second counts now. Mother Blacktooth's confession has bought you this narrow window, but sunrise will bring the full weight of the Crown's manhunt crashing down. One way or another, your fate in Tudor England will be decided in the next few minutes.

The barge lurches closer to the dock pilings, disaster imminent.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Wait for the crash, then row immediately for the far shore
B: Use the distraction to steal a faster boat from the dock
C: Help rescue the drowning sailors to blend in with the crowd
D: Abandon the river plan and flee on foot through the chaos

data.passagePreview: The merchant barge strikes the hidden snag with a thunderous crack of splintering wood. Shouts erupt

data.passage:
The merchant barge strikes the hidden snag with a thunderous crack of splintering wood. Shouts erupt across the water as cargo tumbles into the Thames and sailors scramble to save their vessel. The river gate guards rush toward the commotion, abandoning their posts exactly as you predicted.

"Now!" Will hisses, pushing the stolen skiff into the current. You leap in as he begins rowing with powerful strokes, the little boat cutting through the dark water toward the far shore. Behind you, London erupts in chaos—bells ringing, voices shouting, torches blazing as the manhunt begins in earnest. But you're already halfway across, the city's lights growing distant.

Will's breathing is labored but triumphant. "Your prophecy saved us, Joe. That crash bought us the perfect distraction." The Southwark shore looms ahead, promising safety beyond the Crown's immediate reach. But as you near the muddy bank, reality strikes—you're escaping Tudor London, but you're still trapped in Tudor England. Your strange knowledge has kept you alive, but it's also marked you as something unnatural.

Will beaches the skiff among the reeds. "There's a safe house not far from here," he pants. "But we need to decide what comes next. You can't hide forever, and every prediction you make draws more attention."

The dawn sky is lightening, and across the water, you can see torches moving through London's streets—the witch hunters beginning their search.

A.text: Ask Will to help you find a ship leaving England entirely
A.consequence: Will agrees but warns that ship captains are suspicious of passengers with mysterious knowledge - risk of exposure to foreign agents
A.narration_directive: Will explains the dangers of sea travel and foreign ports, but offers to make contact with smugglers who might take Joe

B.text: Propose using your knowledge to become a traveling fortune-teller
B.consequence: Will sees profit potential but warns that traveling fortune-tellers attract Church attention and accusations of witchcraft
B.narration_directive: Will considers the business opportunity while explaining how the Church hunts fortune-tellers as heretics

C.text: Suggest hiding in the countryside until the hunt dies down
C.consequence: Will agrees this is the safest short-term option and knows hidden locations, but warns it's only temporary
C.narration_directive: Will leads Joe to a remote countryside hideout while discussing how long they can realistically stay hidden

D.text: Consider revealing the truth about your origins to Will
D.consequence: Will reacts with terror and suspicion to the time travel revelation, threatening to turn Joe in as a demon or madman
D.narration_directive: Will's loyalty crumbles as he struggles to comprehend Joe's impossible origins, seeing him as either possessed or insane

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Pending consequences escalate fast: short time windows before they trigger.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
The merchant barge strikes the hidden snag with a thunderous crack of splintering wood. Shouts erupt across the water as cargo tumbles into the Thames and sailors scramble to save their vessel. The river gate guards rush toward the commotion, abandoning their posts exactly as you predicted.

"Now!" Will hisses, pushing the stolen skiff into the current. You leap in as he begins rowing with powerful strokes, the little boat cutting through the dark water toward the far shore. Behind you, London erupts in chaos—bells ringing, voices shouting, torches blazing as the manhunt begins in earnest. But you're already halfway across, the city's lights growing distant.

Will's breathing is labored but triumphant. "Your prophecy saved us, Joe. That crash bought us the perfect distraction." The Southwark shore looms ahead, promising safety beyond the Crown's immediate reach. But as you near the muddy bank, reality strikes—you're escaping Tudor London, but you're still trapped in Tudor England. Your strange knowledge has kept you alive, but it's also marked you as something unnatural.

Will beaches the skiff among the reeds. "There's a safe house not far from here," he pants. "But we need to decide what comes next. You can't hide forever, and every prediction you make draws more attention."

The dawn sky is lightening, and across the water, you can see torches moving through London's streets—the witch hunters beginning their search.

A: Ask Will to help you find a ship leaving England entirely
B: Propose using your knowledge to become a traveling fortune-teller
C: Suggest hiding in the countryside until the hunt dies down
D: Consider revealing the truth about your origins to Will
